content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 19, 2008

Marzia Zafar
577 Castro Street #205
San Francisco, California 94114

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-040

Dear Ms. Zafar:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

As a designated employee of the California Public Utilities Commission, may you make, participate in making or use your position to influence governmental decisions involving your former employer, Sempra Utilities?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Sempra Utilities is not regarded as a "source of income" to you. Therefore, provided you have no other economic interests, you do not have a disqualifying financial interest in any governmental decision involving Sempra Utilities. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not operate to bar you from any decisionmaking activities involving Sempra Utilities.

FACTS

You are currently employed by the California Public Utilities Commission ("PUC"). You were appointed by the Governor, sworn in and started your job at the PUC on June 13, 2007. You were previously employed by Sempra Utilities. Your last day of

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-08-040
Page No. 2

employment with Sempra Utilities was June 12, 2007. Upon receiving the last of any
compensation owed to you by Sempra Utilities on June 12, 2007, you no longer had any
financial ties with Sempra Utilities. Sempra Utilities is an entity regulated by the PUC.
When we spoke on March 14, 2007, you advised that you have no pension or similar
account at Sempra Utilities and you had no expectation of renewed employment with
Sempra at the time you assumed your current position.

ANALYSIS

Your question implicates the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, which prohibit
public officials from making, participating in making, or in any way using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a financial interest.
(Section 87100.) The Commission has adopted an eight-step analysis to determine
whether a public official has a conflict of interest. You acknowledge that as an employee
of PUC, you are a public official governed by these provisions of the Act (Section
82048), and that you will at some point be making, participating in making, or using your
official position to influence governmental decisions involving your former employer
(steps 1 and 2). You wish to clarify, however, whether you have an economic interest in
Sempra Utilities, from whom you had received income as an employee prior to assuming
your position at the PUC (step 3).

An official has a financial interest in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on (among other economic interests) any source of income
amounting to $500 or more that is received within 12 months preceding the decision.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) Sempra Utilities is plainly a "source of income"
to you within the broad meaning of the term. However, Regulation 18703.3(b)
provides an exception for "former employers," as follows:

"(b) Former employers. Source of income, as used in
Government Code section 87103(c) and this section, shall not
include a former employer if: All income from the
employer was received by or accrued to the public official
prior to the time he or she became a public official; the
income was received in the normal course of the previous
employment; and there was no expectation by the public
official at the time he or she assumed office of renewed
employment with the former employer."

Your account of the facts indicates that all income from Sempra Utilities was paid
to or accrued by you prior to the time you became a public official, that this income was
paid in the normal course of your previous employment, and that you have no expectation
of renewed employment with Sempra Utilities. Under these circumstances, Sempra will
not be considered a "source of income" to you, as that term is used within the Act.

File No. A-08-040
Page No. 3

Provided you have no other economic interest in Sempra Utilities, you do not have a
disqualifying financial interest in any governmental decision involving Sempra Utilities.
Therefore, the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not bar you from any
decisionmaking activities that involve Sempra Utilities.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VI:jgl
